column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Iridium Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/metal/non-ferrous/iridium-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for Iridium surface cleaning without thermal damage
Fundamental Nd:YAG wavelength with good Iridium absorption
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control
Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage
Minimum fluence required for effective Iridium surface cleaning
Short pulse duration for efficient ablation with minimal heat diffusion
Optimal scanning speed for efficient area coverage
Recommended number of passes for complete contamination removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning results

Typical range for industrial Nd:YAG laser cleaning of refractory metals like Iridium, starting low due to high reflectivity (~90% at 1064 nm) to avoid excessive heating; max limited to prevent melting (mp 2446°C). Recommended start at 100 W for contamination removal. Source: IPG Photonics specs for YLR series; conservative estimate from metal cleaning studies (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2018).

Effective speeds balance fluence and coverage; slower for heavy contamination (e.g., oxides), faster for light surface prep. Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal Iridium cleaning without thermal buildup, considering low thermal conductivity (14.8 W/m·K). Source: CleanLaser equipment manuals; industry standards for 1064 nm cleaning (Opt. Lasers Eng. 2020).

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective industrial cleaning; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for minimal HAZ on heat-sensitive Iridium, but ns sufficient due to high melting point. Recommended 20 ns to limit heat diffusion. Source: Laser physics reviews (e.g., Prog. Quantum Electron. 2019); ns common in metal ablation studies.

Typical for pulsed cleaning to achieve high average power without excessive heat accumulation; higher rates speed up process but risk thermal effects on reflective Iridium. Recommended 50 kHz balances speed and safety. Source: Academic literature on Nd:YAG cleaning (Appl. Surf. Sci. 2017); trade-off noted in high-rep-rate studies.

Fluence below Iridium damage threshold (~3-6 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to reflectivity); typical for contaminant ablation without substrate damage. Recommended 2 J/cm² for safe removal of light contaminants. Source: Ablation threshold studies for PGMs (J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 2021); conservative for Iridium's properties.

Smaller spots for fine detail on valuable Iridium surfaces, larger for broad areas; 1064 nm beam focused tightly for precision. Recommended 200 μm for uniform cleaning without over-ablation. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf lasers; application-based ranges in industrial cleaning (Lasers Manuf. Mater. Process. 2019).

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy layers; Iridium's durability allows 2 passes without damage. Recommended 2 for complete removal in surface prep. Source: Industry case studies on metal cleaning (e.g., SPIE Proc. 2022); practice for refractory metals.

Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or excessive overlap heating; 50% optimal for Iridium to minimize heat-affected zones while covering reflective surfaces evenly. Source: Process optimization studies (Opt. Express 2018); typical range 30-80% in scanning laser cleaning standards.
